authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — The Tutor Book — OET Writing Comprehensive Rulebook (Sessions 1–10) — adapted for Occupational Therapy (Phase D v1)

rules.title: Six OET letter types
rules.body: Six OET letter types: Routine Referral, Urgent Referral, Discharge Letter, Transfer Letter, Referral to Non-Medical Professional, and Referral to GP.

rules.title: Default is Routine Referral
rules.body: Any referral task without the word 'urgent' = Routine Referral by default.

rules.title: Assessment / management requests are routine
rules.body: Case notes requesting 'assessment' or 'management from a specialist' without 'urgent' = Routine Referral.

rules.title: Urgent identifiers
rules.body: Urgent identifiers: 'urgent referral/assessment', 'admit to hospital', 'hospital admission', 'request acute management', 'as soon as possible (ASAP)', 'acutely unwell', 'for immediate assessment'.

rules.title: Suspected cancer = urgent
rules.body: Suspected cancer ALWAYS applies Urgent Referral rules, even if the word 'urgent' is absent.

rules.title: Discharge letter identifiers
rules.body: Discharge letter identifiers: 'write a discharge letter', patient described as in hospital or being discharged, you are writing from hospital to GP.

rules.title: Non-medical professional list
rules.body: Non-medical professionals: OT, physiotherapist, social worker, psychologist, dietitian, speech pathologist, pharmacist, care facility manager. Nurse is classified as medical, not non-medical.

rules.title: Cancer screening vs suspected cancer
rules.body: Do not confuse routine cancer screening referrals with urgent referrals for suspected cancer.

rules.title: Total exam time 45 minutes
rules.body: Total exam time: 45 minutes.

rules.title: Five-minute reading-only window
rules.body: First 5 minutes = reading time only. No writing is permitted; pen must remain down.

rules.title: Target 30 minutes writing + 10 minutes review
rules.body: Remaining 40 minutes = writing time. Practice target: finish in 30 minutes, leaving 10 minutes for review.

rules.title: Paper layout
rules.body: Paper 1 = case notes (usually 2 pages). Paper 2 / Booklet = writing answer sheet.

rules.title: Written task elements
rules.body: The written task (bottom of case notes) states: letter type, recipient name + address, request, and sometimes the diagnosis.

rules.title: Diagnosis location fallback
rules.body: If diagnosis is absent from the written task, check the 'plan' section immediately above it.

rules.title: Today's date = letter date
rules.body: 'Today's date' at the top of case notes = the date of the letter.

rules.title: Diagnosis types treated identically
rules.body: Diagnosis types — provisional, preliminary, evaluatory, confirmatory — are treated identically in OET letters.

rules.title: Three data categories
rules.body: All data falls into one of three categories: Relevant (must include), Irrelevant (must exclude), Semi-relevant (include if letter is short; exclude if long).

rules.title: Marks for inclusion AND exclusion
rules.body: Marks are awarded for INCLUDING relevant data AND for EXCLUDING irrelevant data. Content selection is the single most important OET writing skill.

rules.title: Recipient specialty drives relevance
rules.body: The recipient specialty determines relevance. Ask: 'Would this specialist need this to manage the patient?'

rules.title: Smoking and drinking always included (except OT)
rules.body: Smoking and drinking: ALWAYS include (positive or negative), EXCEPT when writing to an occupational therapist.

rules.title: Divorce / living situation — conditional
rules.body: Divorce, living situation, number of children: include only if directly relevant to the specialty.

rules.title: Allergy always included for atopic conditions
rules.body: Allergy: ALWAYS include (positive or negative) for atopic conditions (asthma, eczema, hay fever). For non-atopic conditions, include allergy only if relevant to the condition or its medication.

rules.title: Family history — conditional
rules.body: Family history: include only if a family member has a related condition.

rules.title: Letter length guide 180–200 words
rules.body: Letter length guide: 180–200 words (body only). Too long = semi-relevant data included. Too short = relevant data missing.

rules.title: Max 4 body paragraphs
rules.body: Maximum 4 body paragraphs. Typical: 2–3 body paragraphs. Paper-based: fills to approx two-thirds of second page.

rules.title: Letter structure order
rules.body: Letter structure order: Address → [blank] → Date → [blank] → Salutation → Re: line → Introduction → [blank] → Body paragraphs (each separated by blank line) → [blank] → Closure → [blank] → Yours sincerely/faithfully → [1–2 blank lines] → Doctor.

rules.title: No blank line between Salutation and Re: line
rules.body: NO blank line between the Salutation and the Re: line. They are on consecutive lines. Salutation and Re: line are the ONLY two consecutive lines with no blank line between them.

rules.title: Computer-based Enter convention
rules.body: Computer-based exam: two Enter presses = one blank line. 'Space' or 'blank line' throughout this rulebook means 1–2 Enters on the keyboard.

rules.title: One blank line between body paragraphs
rules.body: Each body paragraph is separated from the next by exactly one blank line.

rules.title: Address components
rules.body: Address components: recipient name, specialty, clinic name, street, city/town. Number of lines varies; not always five.

rules.title: Address capitalisation and punctuation
rules.body: Address: first letter of each component = CAPITAL. No commas or full stops. Use Enter (not comma) between components.

rules.title: Address copied from case notes
rules.body: Address is always copied directly from the written task in the case notes.

rules.title: Date = today's visit date
rules.body: Date of the letter = date of today's visit (at top of case notes).

rules.title: Date format consistency
rules.body: Date format must be consistent throughout the letter. Acceptable formats: DD/MM/YYYY, 1 January 2024, 01/01/2024, 1st January 2024.

rules.title: Never abbreviate the year
rules.body: Never abbreviate the year (write 2024, not '24).

rules.title: Leading zero for single-digit dates
rules.body: Numbers 1–9 in dates: always add a leading zero (01, 02 … 09).

rules.title: No 'Date:' prefix
rules.body: Do NOT write the word 'Date:' before the date.

rules.title: One blank line above and below date
rules.body: The date has exactly ONE blank line above it and ONE blank line below it.

rules.title: Standard salutation form
rules.body: Standard: Dear Dr. [Last Name], — capital D, lowercase r. Always use LAST name, never first name.

rules.title: Optional comma / dot
rules.body: Comma after salutation: optional. Dot after Dr/Mr/Ms: optional.

rules.title: No blank line between salutation and Re:
rules.body: No blank line between salutation and Re: line.

rules.title: Special salutations
rules.body: Special salutations: No name given → Dear Doctor. Nurse/OT/Radiographer without name → Dear Sir/Madam. Admissions Officer → Dear Admissions Officer. Emergency Registrar → Dear Emergency Registrar. Assistant Professor → Dear Assistant Professor. UK surgeon → 'Mr [Last Name]' acceptable.

rules.title: Non-medical salutation rules
rules.body: Non-medical professional — no name: Dear Sir/Madam. Name given: Dear Mr/Ms [Last Name].

rules.title: Re: line format
rules.body: Re: line format: Re: [Title] [Full Name] D.O.B: [date]. Both 'Re:' and 'RE:' are acceptable.

rules.title: Re: line full name; body last name only
rules.body: Re: line = full name (first + last). In body: LAST NAME + TITLE only (e.g., Ms Miller). Never repeat full name in body.

rules.title: Age / DOB handling in Re: line
rules.body: When only DOB is given → write DOB in Re: line. When only age is given → write 'aged [X]' (lowercase, no colon) after the full name. Do NOT write the age again in the introduction. When both are given → use DOB in Re: line; age is optional in the introduction.

rules.title: Age forms in running text
rules.body: Age in running text (three acceptable forms): 'Age: 25' / '25 years old' / '25 years of age'.

rules.title: Children: no title; first name in body
rules.body: Children (Day 1 of life up to and including 17 years old): NO title in Re: line — write full name only. In the body: use FIRST NAME only. At 18 years old the patient becomes an adult and receives a title (Mr/Ms/Miss). Never write 'Master' for boys.

rules.title: Yours sincerely vs faithfully
rules.body: 'Yours sincerely' when recipient name is known. 'Yours faithfully' when no name (Dear Sir/Madam, Dear Doctor).

rules.title: Yours sincerely capitalisation
rules.body: Capitalisation: Yours (capital Y), sincerely (lowercase s). Spelling: S-I-N-C-E-R-E-L-Y.

rules.title: Signature 'Doctor' line
rules.body: Write 'Doctor' (capital D) below closing phrase. Allow 1–2 blank lines between closing phrase and 'Doctor'.

rules.title: Introduction concise
rules.body: Introduction must be concise: 2–3 sentences maximum, fewest words possible.

rules.title: Intro must contain identity + purpose
rules.body: Introduction must contain: (a) Unique Identity of the patient, and (b) Purpose / Request.

rules.title: Purpose/Request mandatory
rules.body: Purpose/Request = MOST IMPORTANT element. Never omit it.

rules.title: Unique identity elements
rules.body: Unique identity elements: name, age (if not in header), occupation, summary of main condition.

rules.title: Routine referral intro template
rules.body: Routine referral template: 'I am writing to refer [Title Last Name], a [age]-year-old [occupation], for [specialist] assessment and management of [condition].'
exemplarPhrases: I am writing to refer {title} {lastName}, a {age}-year-old {occupation}, for {specialist} assessment and management of {condition}.

rules.title: Urgent intro must contain 'urgent'
rules.body: Urgent referral: MUST contain the word 'urgent' — 'I am writing to urgently refer...' / 'I am writing to request urgent assessment of...'

rules.title: Discharge: no patient identity in intro
rules.body: Discharge letter to GP: Do NOT describe patient identity in introduction — GP knows the patient.

rules.title: Discharge intro template
rules.body: Discharge introduction template: 'I am writing to update you regarding Mr Jones, who underwent [procedure] at [hospital] and is now ready for discharge, for whom I am requesting your ongoing care.'
exemplarPhrases: I am writing to update you regarding {title} {lastName}, who underwent {procedure} at {hospital} and is now ready for discharge, for whom I am requesting your ongoing care.

rules.title: Appositive uses gentleman/lady
rules.body: Appositive clause: 'Ms Miller, a 25-year-old teacher, presented with...' — use 'gentleman' for males, 'lady' for females (not man/woman).

rules.title: Minimum 2 body paragraphs
rules.body: Minimum 2 body paragraphs. NEVER write the entire letter in one paragraph.

rules.title: Today's visit has its own paragraph
rules.body: Today's visit must always have its own paragraph. Exception: only one item of information today → may combine with adjacent visit.

rules.title: Previous visits combined or summarised
rules.body: Previous visits may be combined or summarised. Do NOT give every visit its own paragraph.

rules.title: Subjective + Objective + Management
rules.body: Visit structure: Subjective (symptoms) + Objective (examination) + Management.

rules.title: Urgent referral: body starts with today's visit
rules.body: Urgent referral: ALWAYS begin body with TODAY'S VISIT (most acute presentation), then background chronologically.

rules.title: Prefer elapsed time over exact dates
rules.body: Visit referencing: prefer elapsed time over exact dates — '3 months ago' preferred over the exact date.

rules.title: Never 'next visit'
rules.body: Next visit reference: 'on the following visit' / 'later on' / 'later that month'. NEVER 'next visit'.

rules.title: Never write today's date in body
rules.body: NEVER write today's date in the body. Use 'On today's visit' or 'On today's presentation' instead.

rules.title: No 'Yesterday'
rules.body: 'Yesterday' never used in a medical letter. Write 'one day prior' or 'one day ago' instead.

rules.title: 'Over the last X' starts a paragraph
rules.body: 'Over the last [X time]': summarises multiple visits with the same complaint. Requires present perfect. Use only to START a paragraph.

rules.title: Duration adjective hyphenation
rules.body: Duration as adjective (before noun): hyphenate, no plural: 'a 6-month history of acne'. Duration standalone: plural: 'For 6 months, she has had acne.'

rules.title: Condition change vocabulary
rules.body: Condition unchanged: 'persisted' / 'remained unchanged'. Improved: 'there was partial improvement'. Worsened: 'deteriorated' / 'worsened'.

rules.title: Advice vocabulary
rules.body: Advice: 'She was advised to adopt lifestyle changes.' / 'She was counselled regarding...' (never 'instructed to adopt').

rules.title: Never 'the patient'
rules.body: In body: last name + title only (Ms Miller). Never use full name again. Never use 'the patient'.

rules.title: Name vs pronoun pattern
rules.body: Name vs. Pronoun within paragraphs: Use the patient's name (title + last name for adults; first name for children) at the FIRST mention in each paragraph. For ALL subsequent mentions within the SAME paragraph, use a pronoun. At the start of EVERY new paragraph, use the name again.

rules.title: Standard closure phrases
rules.body: Standard phrases: 'I trust this information will be of use.' / 'Please do not hesitate to contact me should you require further information.'
exemplarPhrases: I trust this information will be of use.
Please do not hesitate to contact me should you require further information.

rules.title: Urgent closure: 'at your earliest convenience'
rules.body: Urgent closure MUST include 'at your earliest convenience.' Spelling: C-O-N-V-E-N-I-E-N-C-E.

rules.title: Use 'urgent' once
rules.body: Use 'urgent' once in introduction only; use 'at your earliest convenience' in closure to imply urgency — do not repeat 'urgent' throughout.

rules.title: Follow-up / review date must appear
rules.body: Follow-up / review date stated in case notes → ALWAYS mention in closure.

rules.title: Enclosed results instead of dumping values
rules.body: Copy of results/imaging stated as enclosed → write 'Please find enclosed a copy of Mr [Name]'s pathology/imaging results.' Do NOT also list the values in the letter.

rules.title: Medication list attachment
rules.body: Copy of medication list attached → list medication names only (no doses).

rules.title: Patient-initiated referral
rules.body: Patient-initiated referral → 'upon his/her request' or 'at Mr Jones' request' in introduction or closure.

rules.title: Consent statement
rules.body: Consent in case notes → 'Please note that Mr Jones has been fully informed of his diagnosis and management.'

rules.title: Blank line before closing phrase
rules.body: Always leave ONE blank line before 'Yours sincerely,' / 'Yours faithfully,'. The last body paragraph and the closing phrase must be separated by a blank line.

rules.title: Intro tense options
rules.body: Introduction: present simple or present perfect (both acceptable).

rules.title: Visits = past simple
rules.body: All visits (today and previous): PAST SIMPLE. Safest choice; never wrong for visit content.

rules.title: Present perfect only if symptom persists
rules.body: Present perfect for a visit: acceptable only if the symptom is still present at time of writing. CANNOT use present perfect for vital signs.

rules.title: Ongoing facts = present simple
rules.body: Medical/social/family history — ongoing facts: present simple. 'She has hypertension.'

rules.title: 'Since' + starting point = present perfect
rules.body: 'Since' + starting point → present perfect: 'She has had hypertension since 2010.'

rules.title: 'For' + duration = present perfect
rules.body: 'For' + duration → present perfect: 'She has had hypertension for 5 years.'

rules.title: Smoking/drinking tense
rules.body: Smoking/drinking with duration → present perfect continuous: 'She has been smoking for 20 years.' Without duration → present simple: 'She smokes.'

rules.title: Surgery = past simple
rules.body: Resolved conditions and surgery → PAST SIMPLE. 'She had a cholecystectomy in 2018.' Never use present perfect for surgery.

rules.title: 'Over the last X' first sentence = present perfect
rules.body: 'Over the last [X]': first sentence → present perfect; remaining sentences in same paragraph → past simple acceptable.

rules.title: 'X ago' = past simple
rules.body: '[X] ago' → always past simple. 'She has presented 3 weeks ago' = WRONG. 'She presented 3 weeks ago' = CORRECT.

rules.title: Medical records present simple
rules.body: Medical records: 'Mr Jones' medical records reveal/show that he has [condition].' Use present simple for reveal/show.

rules.title: Avoid past perfect
rules.body: Past perfect (had + past participle): AVOID in OET. Alternative: ING form or noun.

rules.title: Reported speech uses ING or noun
rules.body: After reported/mentioned/stated/described: use ING or noun. 'She reported having headaches.' NOT '...that she had had headaches.'

rules.title: 'Next visit' → 'on the following visit'
rules.body: 'Next visit' in past narrative → 'on the following visit.' Never use 'next.'

rules.title: Translate Latin abbreviations
rules.body: Latin abbreviations MUST be translated: od/om → once a day; bd/bid → twice a day; tds/tid → three times a day; qds/qid → four times a day; stat → immediately; prn → as needed.

rules.title: Single medication format
rules.body: Single medication: Drug, dose. Example: 'Panadol, 500 mg.'

rules.title: Multiple medications format
rules.body: Multiple medications: Drug, dose; Drug, dose; and Drug, dose. (comma: drug-dose; semicolon: between pairs; 'and' before last drug.)

rules.title: Trade vs generic capitalisation
rules.body: Trade name: Capitalise (Panadol, Augmentin). Generic: lowercase (paracetamol, amoxicillin). Use one type consistently.

rules.title: Routes conventions
rules.body: Routes: 'oral' in full (never 'PO'); IM, IV, SC, SL abbreviations acceptable. Route before drug: 'oral Panadol, 500 mg.'

rules.title: Elevated / reduced phrasing
rules.body: Elevated values: 'an elevation in glucose' / 'an increased glucose level' / 'elevated glucose.' Reduced: 'a reduction in glucose' / 'a decreased glucose level.' Do not write 'a glucose' alone.

rules.title: Level article rules
rules.body: 'Glucose level' can take article: 'an increased glucose level.' Plural modifier: no article: 'increased white cell counts.'

rules.title: Include numerical values with units
rules.body: Always include numerical values with units when given. Example: 'glucose of 7.8 mmol/L.' Never write the reference range.

rules.title: 'an MRI' / 'an X-ray'
rules.body: 'an MRI' and 'an X-ray' — both take 'an' (initial vowel sound from letter name).

rules.title: Numerals with units
rules.body: Numerals with units always: '38.5°C', '180/110 mmHg', '500 mg.'

rules.title: Clinic scheduling fractions
rules.body: Clinic scheduling fractions: 1/4 = once a week; 1/12 = once a year; 3/52 = three times a year; 3/7 = three times a week; 8/24 = eight-hourly; 12/24 = twice a day.

rules.title: No contractions
rules.body: NO contractions: 'do not' not 'don't'; 'I am' not 'I'm'; 'cannot' not 'can't'.

rules.title: Never 'patient' in body
rules.body: Never use the word 'patient' in the body. Use last name + title, or a pronoun.

rules.title: No 'smoker/drinker' labels
rules.body: Never use 'smoker/drinker' as labels. Write 'she smokes' / 'she drinks alcohol.'

rules.title: Replace colloquial terms
rules.body: Replace 'tired' with 'fatigue.' Replace 'bad' with 'poor' or 'inadequate.' No colloquial language.

rules.title: Conditions lowercase
rules.body: Medical conditions: LOWERCASE. bronchial asthma, myocardial infarction, hypertension, gastroenteritis. Capitalising these is an error.

rules.title: Eponymous diseases capitalised
rules.body: Named (eponymous) diseases: CAPITALS. Parkinson's, Crohn's, Alzheimer's, Graves', Doppler.

rules.title: Trade names and abbreviations
rules.body: Trade medication names: capitalise. Bacteria: first word capital, second lowercase (Staphylococcus aureus). Abbreviations: all capitals (MRI, CT, ECG, A&E).

rules.title: Numbers 1–9 as words
rules.body: Numbers 1–9 in running text: write as words. Numbers 10+: numerals. Exception: always numerals with units ('500 mg').

rules.title: 'However' punctuation
rules.body: 'However': always preceded by semicolon; comma follows. Pattern: '[clause]; however, [clause].'

rules.title: 'Therefore' / 'Thus' punctuation
rules.body: 'Therefore' / 'Thus': preceded by semicolon; comma follows. Pattern: '[clause]; therefore, [clause].'

rules.title: 'In addition' punctuation
rules.body: 'In addition' (joining two complete clauses): semicolon before + comma after.

rules.title: 'In addition to' takes noun/ING
rules.body: 'In addition to' / 'together with' / 'along with' / 'as well as': NO preceding punctuation; followed by noun or ING.

rules.title: Proximity rule
rules.body: Proximity rule (negative sentence with 'or'): verb agrees with CLOSEST subject.

rules.title: Reverse rule
rules.body: Reverse rule ('along with' / 'together with' / 'in addition to'): verb agrees with word BEFORE the linker.

rules.title: Despite vs although
rules.body: 'Despite' + noun/ING (not a full clause). 'Although' + full clause. Both acceptable.

rules.title: 'As' (because) comma
rules.body: 'As' (meaning because) in mid-sentence: comma before.

rules.title: 'For which' comma
rules.body: 'For which': comma before.

rules.title: Coordinating adjectives
rules.body: Coordinating adjectives (same noun): comma between them.

rules.title: Sentence length 15–25 words
rules.body: Sentence length: 15–25 words target. Do not chain 3+ clauses. No comma splices.

rules.title: Max 1–2 linkers per paragraph
rules.body: Max 1–2 linkers per paragraph. Excessive linkers reduce Conciseness and Clarity score.

rules.title: Appositive commas
rules.body: Appositive: between two commas. Use 'gentleman' / 'lady' (not man/woman).

rules.title: No patient labels
rules.body: Never label patients: 'anxious', 'non-compliant.' Write: 'she expressed concern about...' / 'he reported difficulty adhering to...'

rules.title: Three elements change for urgent
rules.body: Three elements change in an urgent referral: (1) Introduction contains 'urgent', (2) Closure contains 'at your earliest convenience', (3) Body starts with today's visit.

rules.title: Urgent intro must contain 'urgent'
rules.body: Introduction MUST contain 'urgent'.

rules.title: Urgent closure phrase
rules.body: Closure MUST include 'at your earliest convenience.' Do not repeat 'urgent' after the introduction.

rules.title: Urgent body starts today
rules.body: Body: ALWAYS start with TODAY'S VISIT (acute presentation), then background chronologically.

rules.title: Urgent structure
rules.body: Structure: P1 = today's emergency. P2+ = background history. Final paragraph = active medical conditions + current medications.

rules.title: Ongoing conditions on active meds must be mentioned
rules.body: Any ongoing medical condition with active medications MUST be mentioned, even if unrelated to the urgent issue.

rules.title: Exclude resolved conditions
rules.body: Fully resolved conditions with no current medications: do NOT include.

rules.title: Penicillin allergy conditional
rules.body: Penicillin allergy: include only if patient is receiving or needs antibiotics.

rules.title: Omit empty PMH paragraph
rules.body: No past medical history? Simply omit that paragraph.

rules.title: 'ASAP' → 'at your earliest convenience'
rules.body: 'ASAP' in case notes → write 'at your earliest convenience' in the letter. Never write 'ASAP.'

rules.title: A&E and ED capitalised
rules.body: A&E and ED: always capitalised (A&E, ED, Emergency Department).

rules.title: Hospital doctor → GP
rules.body: Written by hospital doctor to GP; updates GP on admission and discharge.

rules.title: Discharge intro template
rules.body: Introduction: 'I am writing to update you regarding Mr Jones, who underwent [procedure] at [hospital] and is now ready for discharge, for whom I am requesting your ongoing care.' NEVER use routine referral language.

rules.title: Discharge: no identity in intro
rules.body: Do NOT describe patient identity in introduction — GP knows the patient.

rules.title: Discharge omits FH/SH/smoking/PMH/occupation
rules.body: Do NOT include: family history, social history, smoking/drinking, previous medical history, occupation. GP knows all of this.

rules.title: Discharge allergy exception
rules.body: Mention allergy only if it directly influenced treatment.

rules.title: Admission phrasing
rules.body: Admission: 'Mr Jones was admitted to [hospital] with [symptoms/condition].' Past simple. NEVER 'was presented' (incorrect passive).

rules.title: Discharge plan is most important
rules.body: Discharge plan paragraph = most important. Must include: discharge medications with doses + post-discharge instructions.

rules.title: Discharge meds format
rules.body: Discharge medications: same format — Drug, dose; Drug, dose and Drug, dose.

rules.title: List all admission investigations (incl. normal)
rules.body: All investigations performed during admission (including normal results) MUST be listed with values.

rules.title: Follow-up dates mandatory in discharge
rules.body: Follow-up / review dates: ALWAYS include if stated in case notes.

rules.title: Discharge consent
rules.body: Consent: 'Please note that Mr Jones has been fully informed of his diagnosis and management.'

rules.title: Treatment FOR, not FROM
rules.body: 'Treatment FOR' not 'Treatment FROM.' Always: treated for, admitted for, referred for, managed for.

rules.title: Discharge past tense throughout
rules.body: Past tense throughout (all events occurred during admission).

rules.title: Enclose results reference
rules.body: Copy of results stated as enclosed → 'Please find enclosed a copy of Mr [Name]'s pathology results.' Do not list values.

rules.title: Non-medical list
rules.body: Non-medical professionals: OT, physiotherapist, social worker, psychologist, dietitian, speech pathologist, pharmacist, care facility manager. Nurse is medical.

rules.title: Translate all jargon
rules.body: Do NOT use medical jargon. Translate: 'hypertension' → 'high blood pressure'; avoid abbreviations like 'BP' unless previously spelled out.

rules.title: Non-medical salutation
rules.body: Salutation: Dear Sir/Madam (no name). Dear Mr/Ms [Last Name] (name given).

rules.title: Non-medical content
rules.body: Include: condition in plain language, functional impact, specific professional request, relevant social context.

rules.title: Exclude out-of-scope details
rules.body: Exclude: medical details irrelevant to the professional's scope.

rules.title: Family dentist = specialist-style
rules.body: Family dentist: does NOT know the patient — treat like a specialist; include relevant background.

rules.title: Imperative plan items = for GP
rules.body: Imperative items in the plan = for GP. Items on the referral request line = for the specialist. Never ask the specialist to perform GP tasks.

rules.title: Mental health referral conventions
rules.body: Mental health referrals: correlate symptoms across visits; include review date; include consent statement if mentioned; include 'upon his/her request' if patient initiated.

rules.title: Six marking criteria
rules.body: Six marking criteria: (1) Purpose — max 3 marks; (2) Content — max 7; (3) Conciseness & Clarity — max 7; (4) Genre & Style — max 7; (5) Organisation & Layout — max 7; (6) Language — max 7.

rules.title: Purpose 0–3 scale only
rules.body: Purpose is the ONLY criterion scored 0–3. All others are scored 0–7.

rules.title: Content + Conciseness dominate pass/fail
rules.body: Most heavily weighted for pass/fail: Content and Conciseness & Clarity.

rules.title: Genre and Style significant
rules.body: Genre and Style has significant impact on the overall result.

rules.title: Language does not need to be perfect
rules.body: Language does not need to be perfect. Most OET failures result from poor content selection, not grammar errors.

rules.title: Target Band 6+
rules.body: Required score: approximately Band 6+ in most criteria; at least Band 2 out of 3 in Purpose.

rules.title: Real-clinic test
rules.body: Examiner standard: 'Could this letter be sent in a real clinical setting?' Yes = pass. No = fail.

rules.title: Assessment is holistic
rules.body: Assessment is holistic. Do not attempt to calculate an exact score.

adult.reLine: Title + full name (e.g. Re: Ms Sara Miller D.O.B: 01/01/1984)
adult.bodyFirstMention: Title + last name (e.g. Ms Miller presented with...)

child.reLine: Full name only, NO title (e.g. Re: Sara Miller D.O.B: 01/01/2015)
child.bodyFirstMention: First name only (e.g. Sara presented with...)

ageOnly.reLine: Re: Ms Sara Miller, aged 40 (lowercase 'aged', no colon, no 'Age:' prefix)

letterSkeleton: Body paragraphs (one blank line between each, 2–4 total)

professionSpecific.label: Occupational Therapy
professionSpecific.notes: Functional capacity, activities of daily living (ADLs), home environment and equipment needs drive content.
